workflows.name: Seasonal planning, dispatch, execution, and verification
workflows.purpose: Convert annual objectives, seasonal signals, and field exceptions into accountable block-level work with defensible closure and actual-use records.
workflows.frequency: Daily during active operations and replanned whenever conditions change
triggers: Budgeted recurring work reaches its window
Weather, winery, certification, or equipment constraint changes
inputs: Stable block identity and map
Growth stage and current block condition
Annual plan, budget, and work history
Labor, contractor, equipment, and material availability
Weather and applicable restrictions
preconditions: The operational block is unambiguously identified
Safety, compliance, and timing constraints are current
Accountable work ownership and completion evidence are defined

steps.description: A seasonal, field, weather, winery, certification, or equipment signal is recorded against a stable block identity.
The accountable specialist defines whether work is needed, its scope, timing, method, and operational constraints.
The manager approves, revises, escalates, or declines the proposed work according to cost, safety, compliance, and delegated authority.
The manager or coordinator assigns the block, priority, crew, equipment, materials, timing, and evidence required for closure.
The foreman acknowledges the current assignment and confirms that field instructions and constraints are understood.
The crew executes the work while preserving progress, actual resource use, and exception context at block or row scope.
The foreman reports actual scope, labor, equipment, materials, completion status, and any unresolved exception without flattening partial work.
The manager verifies field scope and actuals before closure data flows to progress, payroll, compliance, and cost records.

decisions.question: Does the signal require action now, later, or no action?
Does the work need explicit approval before assignment?
What closure state accurately represents the field result?

outputs: Actual labor, equipment, material, and acreage quantities
handoffs: Viticulturist or field observer to vineyard manager
Vineyard manager or coordinator to foreman or contractor
Foreman to field crew
Field completion and exceptions back to management
Verified actuals to payroll, costing, compliance, and progress reporting
approvals: Routine work may use delegated approval
Pesticide, major purchase, contractor, certification-sensitive, and harvest work uses stronger approval
exceptions: Priority or weather changes after dispatch
Labor, equipment, material, or contractor unavailable
workarounds: Verbal or phone redispatch followed by later record entry
Split affected rows or acreage while preserving one accountable history
Manual reconciliation of field notes with office records
risks: Actual costs and compliance records disconnected from execution
source_anchors: ## Detailed workflow cards and handoff maps
**Workflow card: seasonal work planning and work-order dispatch**
**Typical sequence.** Inspect or receive a field signal; decide whether action is required
Managers need “done/not done/why not” visibility across estates and crews.
field_validation_questions: Which routine work can foremen self-authorize and which decisions require manager approval?
How are partial completion and actual quantities reported today across estate and contractor crews?
When work changes mid-day, which record is treated as authoritative?

workflows.name: Scouting, recommendation, crop protection, and application records
workflows.purpose: Turn monitored pest or disease evidence into a safe, timely, compliant intervention and a defensible application and efficacy history.
workflows.frequency: Scheduled monitoring and event-driven treatment throughout susceptible periods
inputs: Stable block and mapped observation identity
Monitoring record, trap result, symptom, and weather risk
Treatment threshold and target pest or disease
Current label, regulation, buyer, certification, REI, and PHI rules
Product inventory, calibration, applicator qualification, PPE, and forecast
preconditions: Block and treatment area are accurate
The product and use are allowed
Weather, exclusion zone, PPE, REI, and PHI prechecks pass
A qualified handler and calibrated method are available

steps.description: The scout monitors the identified block and records pressure, symptoms, trap counts, mapped areas, and conditions.
The viticulturist compares the observation with risk and threshold evidence and selects action or continued monitoring.
The crop-protection lead verifies material, method, calibration, wind, sensitive areas, worker protections, REI, PHI, and buyer rules.
The approved plan enters a release check for product, calibration, weather, worker presence, REI, PHI, and sensitive-area constraints.
The crop-protection lead releases the current application only after the application-time preflight remains valid.
The qualified handler performs the application within approved conditions and records actual date, location, product, rate, method, and operator.
Required application information, worker protections, and restricted-entry notice are posted or made accessible.
The compliance coordinator updates spray diary, buyer or certification formats, and jurisdictional reports from the field application record.
The viticulturist performs and records follow-up monitoring to assess efficacy and determine further action.

decisions.question: Has treatment pressure or risk reached the action threshold?
Is the selected product and method allowed for this block and target?
Do weather, worker, calibration, REI, PHI, and sensitive-area prechecks permit execution?
Did follow-up monitoring show acceptable efficacy?
Is the application, restriction, posting, diary, and use-report record complete and attributable for reporting?

outputs: Regulatory, buyer, and certification diary entries
handoffs: Scout to viticulturist
Viticulturist to crop-protection lead or manager
Approved plan to pesticide handler and foreman
Application facts to workers and compliance coordinator
Diary or report to buyer, certifier, or regulator
Follow-up result back to viticulturist and manager
approvals: Crop-protection authorization after legal, safety, agronomic, and commercial prechecks
Organization-specific manager approval for treatment or material commitment
exceptions: Wind or rain outside allowable conditions
workarounds: Reschedule application
Split treatment area
Select an allowed alternative product or method
Make attributed manual diary corrections
Maintain separate buyer-specific diaries where common formats do not exist
source_anchors: **Workflow card: scouting to spray execution and compliance record**
**Step sequence.** Monitor and keep records; confirm pressure and treatment threshold
UC IPM explicitly calls for monitoring records, calibration, weather checks, REI/PHI review, application recording, and follow-up efficacy checks.
EPA WPS requires worker protections, handler instructions, PPE, central posting, and access to application information.
field_validation_questions: Who has final treatment authority in each operating organization?
Which buyer, certification, and jurisdictional diaries must be generated from one application?
How are cancellations, split blocks, and late corrections currently preserved?
Which efficacy thresholds are used by pest, disease, block, and growth stage?

workflows.name: Irrigation scheduling, fertility adjustment, and delivery verification
workflows.purpose: Translate plant, soil, weather, and nutrient signals into block water or fertility action with verified delivery and resource-use history.
workflows.frequency: Recurring irrigation cycles with event-driven adjustment for stress, heat, malfunction, and tissue results
inputs: Stable block and irrigation-zone identity
Soil moisture, evapotranspiration, weather, and plant-status observations
Growth stage, target vigor or style, and block history
Tissue or petiole analysis and prior nutrient inputs
System readiness, water availability, and applicable resource plan
preconditions: Block and irrigation zone relationships are current
Sensor, observation, and laboratory timing are known
Delivery system and input inventory are available for the proposed action

steps.description: Current block, plant, soil, system, weather, and recent-delivery conditions are recorded against the stable block identity.
The viticulturist recommends an irrigation or fertility adjustment with timing, quantity, method, and review conditions.
The manager approves the agronomic adjustment within current resource, certification, and operating constraints.
The irrigation lead assigns executable valve, set, duration, fertigation, and completion instructions to the responsible irrigator.
The irrigator begins delivery and records observed system behavior, changes, interruptions, and actual timing.
The irrigator records actual delivery, affected area, and any partial or uneven result rather than only planned duration.
The irrigation lead verifies delivery evidence and routes water, nutrient, malfunction, and sustainability totals to their consumers.

decisions.question: What water action is warranted by current block and weather evidence?
Do tissue results and recent inputs warrant a fertility adjustment?
Was the planned delivery achieved evenly and completely?
Is the recommended irrigation or fertility action approved within current resource, certification, and operating constraints?

handoffs: Sensor, field, or lab result to viticulturist
Viticulturist recommendation to irrigation lead and manager
Irrigation lead to irrigator
Delivery result and malfunction back to irrigation lead
Verified totals to sustainability and management reporting
approvals: Vineyard manager or delegated viticulturist approval for material or plan changes where required
exceptions: Lab result arrives after action window
Block reality conflicts with office assumptions
workarounds: Manual irrigator update
Split or repeat a set
Field inspection before accepting sensor evidence
Later reconciliation of estimated and actual delivery
risks: Weak linkage to later yield or quality
source_anchors: **Workflow card: irrigation scheduling and fertility adjustment**
**Typical sequence.** Review recent block and weather conditions; inspect irrigation system or receive irrigator update
**Pain points.** Limited visibility into actual delivery
water-use totals and nitrogen-use totals for certification or management reporting
field_validation_questions: What measurements and thresholds actually cause irrigation changes by region and growth stage?
How is actual delivery verified when telemetry is absent or unreliable?
Who may change fertility inputs and what approval or compliance checks apply?
How are irrigation zones mapped when they do not align with operational blocks?

workflows.name: Crop estimation and forecast revision
workflows.purpose: Produce a traceable block-level crop forecast, revise it as evidence changes, and communicate the operational and commercial consequences.
workflows.frequency: Repeated estimates from crop set through harvest, followed by actual-yield reconciliation
triggers: Lag phase or veraison estimate window
Visible block variation or crop-thinning question
inputs: Stable block identity and productive vine count
Representative vine and cluster sample
Bunches per vine and cluster weight
Historical block estimates and harvest weights
Current condition, style, contract, and winery-capacity context
preconditions: Sampling method and date are recorded
Block acreage and vine population are current
Historical estimates and actuals resolve to the same stable block

steps.description: The viticulturist samples representative vines and clusters using a recorded method and block context.
The viticulturist calculates a block forecast and interprets variation against historical and current-season evidence.
The manager reviews the estimate, sampling confidence, crop-load implications, and downstream planning consequences.
The approved forecast is published to winery, grower, labor, transport, container, budget, and contract planning consumers.
The final harvest actual is linked to the published forecast without replacing the estimate or its assumptions.
The manager reconciles forecast versions with the harvest actual and records error, learning, and downstream planning consequences.

decisions.question: Is the sample representative enough to publish a block estimate?
Does the estimate require crop-load action or a planning change?
Does new evidence materially change the current forecast?

outputs: Dated block crop estimate with method and confidence
Winery, labor, logistics, contract, and budget planning update
handoffs: Field sample to viticulturist
Forecast to manager and grower-relations
Approved estimate to grower, winery, labor, logistics, and finance
Harvest actuals back to viticulture and management planning
approvals: Manager approval of the operational forecast
Shared grower-winery acknowledgment where contracts or planning require it
exceptions: Block identity or vine count mismatch
workarounds: Communicate a range instead of false precision
Resample affected subareas
Manually reconcile aliases and historical weights
Revise spreadsheets, email, or phone forecasts separately
risks: Labor, bin, truck, tank, or revenue plan based on wrong tonnage
source_anchors: | 3 | Crop estimation and forecast revision |
**Workflow card: crop estimation and maturity tracking to harvest-date decision**
**Typical sequence.** Count and sample representative vines; update forecast
Accuracy improves only if historical and current-season records stay connected at block level.
field_validation_questions: Which sampling formulas, timing rules, and confidence conventions are used by operation and variety?
Who approves and receives each estimate revision?
What magnitude of change triggers operational replanning or contractual notice?
How are estimate errors reviewed after harvest?

workflows.name: Maturity sampling, winery communication, and pick decision
workflows.purpose: Connect representative field sampling, laboratory and sensory evidence, winery capacity, and operating constraints to an attributable pick decision.
workflows.frequency: Weekly after veraison, increasing to daily or near-daily as harvest approaches
triggers: Weather, rot, smoke, or capacity risk
inputs: Stable block and sample identity
Representative berry or cluster sample
Brix, pH, TA, flavor, fruit condition, and trend
Weather and hazard outlook
Labor, container, trucking, tank, delivery-slot, contract, and quality constraints
preconditions: Sampling method, location, collector, and time are attributable
Laboratory results resolve to the field sample and block
Pick authority and decision participants are known for estate and contract fruit

steps.description: The assigned sampler collects a representative, identified berry or cluster sample and records field flavor and condition observations.
The traceable sample enters laboratory custody with expected timing and chain-of-identity intact.
The laboratory processes the linked sample and returns Brix, pH, TA, and other agreed results.
The viticulturist interprets the trend with sensory condition, weather, crop state, and forecast evidence.
Grower-relations communicates a pick proposal or continued-sampling recommendation with current evidence and constraints.
The authorized winery and vineyard participants negotiate or confirm pick timing, quality target, and delivery conditions.
The authorized pick becomes a dated labor, container, truck, and winery-slot commitment.

decisions.question: Is the sample and result representative and current enough for a readiness decision?
Does maturity, flavor, condition, and trend support proposing a pick?
Who holds final pick authority for this fruit and contract?
Can the proposed date satisfy quality, weather, labor, tank, and delivery constraints?

handoffs: Field sampler to laboratory
Laboratory to viticulturist, grower-relations, grower, and winemaker
Viticulturist recommendation to decision participants
Authorized pick to vineyard manager, harvest crew, trucking, and winery intake
approvals: Final pick authority follows the estate, buyer, or contract arrangement
Shared grower-winery agreement is common even where one party holds final authority
exceptions: Rapid rot, weather, heat, or smoke change
Labor, tank, or delivery slot unavailable
workarounds: Increase sample frequency
Repeat or parallel sampling
Negotiate an alternate date or delivery condition
Use phone or email to resolve timing and record the decision later
risks: Pick decision based on stale or mismatched evidence
Fruit quality loss while participants reconcile conflicting data
Downstream schedule commitments made before authorization
source_anchors: | 6 | Maturity sampling and winery communication |
**Workflow card: crop estimation and maturity tracking to harvest-date decision**
as harvest nears, increase sample frequency; send results to winemaker, manager, grower, or buyer as relevant
many arrangements rely on communication and mutual agreement even where final pick timing sits with the winery
field_validation_questions: Who holds final pick authority for every estate, grower, buyer, and contract pattern?
What constitutes an acceptable sample and how long is a result current?
How are conflicting field and winery measurements adjudicated?
Which verbal or phone decisions are later reconstructed, by whom, and from what evidence?

workflows.name: Harvest planning, execution, loads, intake, quality, and reconciliation
workflows.purpose: Move authorized fruit from the correct block to the correct intake slot and turn each physical load into accepted, adjusted, or rejected commercial truth.
workflows.frequency: Per authorized pick, crew shift, and delivered load during harvest
triggers: Weigh or quality record requires reconciliation
inputs: Authorized pick and block identity
Crew, machine or hand method, containers, and harvest conditions
Truck and delivery slot
Sample identity, quality tolerances, contract, and buyer instructions
Weigh bill requirements and settlement terms
preconditions: Pick authorization and delivery slot are current
Block, grower, variety, sample, load, and destination identities are linked
Crew, containers, transport, and intake capacity are confirmed

steps.description: The manager converts final pick authorization into an accountable crew, container, equipment, transport, block, timing, and delivery-slot plan.
The harvest crew executes the authorized block and conditions while preserving container and source-block identity.
Transportation carries the identified load and its documentation to the assigned winery intake within the agreed window.
Winery intake verifies load and block identity, records weight, and preserves the intake sample and condition evidence.
The authorized winery role records acceptance, downgrade, or rejection against agreed tolerances without changing the observed intake evidence.
Winery intake enters the accepted delivery with load, weight, quality, grower, vineyard, block, and timing identifiers.
Grower-relations compares the vineyard, transport, intake, buyer-system, quality, and contract records and opens a dispute or correction when they differ.
Finance uses the accepted and reconciled record set to produce settlement inputs while preserving later adjustment authority and history.

decisions.question: Which harvest method and conditions satisfy quality, timing, labor, and cost constraints?
Does the arriving load identity and intake evidence match the authorized fruit?
Does the load meet agreed quality and delivery tolerances?
Is a weigh, identity, grade, or delivery entry disputed or incorrect?
Do accepted records and contract terms support settlement?

outputs: Accepted, downgraded, or rejected load record
Harvest summary, crop accounting, and settlement input
handoffs: Pick authority to vineyard manager
Manager to harvest crew and transportation
Vineyard load to winery intake
Intake disposition to grower, grower-relations, manager, and winemaker
Weigh documentation to crop accounting and finance
Dispute evidence between grower and winery
approvals: Final pick authorization
Winery acceptance, downgrade, or rejection
Commercial correction or settlement adjustment
exceptions: Winery delay or changed tank capacity
Hot fruit or excessive material other than grapes
workarounds: Temporary alternate delivery
Emergency sorting
Repricing or downgrade negotiation
Manual correction of weigh entries
Seek another buyer when notice and contract allow
risks: Wrong fruit attributed to a contract
source_anchors: | 1 | Harvest decision, execution, delivery, and reconciliation |
**Workflow card: harvest execution to weigh-ticket and payment reconciliation**
the grower indicates grower number, variety, vineyard and block on the weigh bill
the processor enters the weigh bill within 72 hours, and the grower reviews the entry for accuracy
field_validation_questions: Which identifier is physically attached to each container or load and when can it change?
What evidence and authority govern acceptance, downgrade, and rejection?
How are urgent delivery-slot changes communicated and acknowledged?
Who can correct a weigh or grade record and how does that correction affect settlement?

workflows.name: Crew scheduling, attendance, time, payroll, and cost allocation
workflows.purpose: Connect planned block work and safety obligations to attributable crew attendance, verified task progress, lawful pay inputs, and enterprise cost allocation.
workflows.frequency: Per shift and work assignment, with payroll-period and management reconciliation
triggers: Attendance, break, piece-rate, or completion exception
inputs: Approved work assignments and stable block identity
Crew roster, qualifications, language, and contractor relationship
Shift, attendance, breaks, and applicable pay rules
Task progress, acreage, units, or piece-rate quantities
Cost center, labor class, and budget context
preconditions: Workers and crews are attributable without exposing unnecessary personal data
Work assignments identify block, activity, and accountable foreman
Current pay, break, safety, and contractor rules are available

steps.description: The coordinator schedules a qualified crew or contractor against block work, timing, safety, and labor demand.
The foreman confirms the roster, qualification, instructions, and safety briefing before work begins.
The foreman records attendance, task changes, breaks, quantities, and attributable work as the crew executes.
The foreman submits time, piece-rate units, task quantities, block allocation, and unresolved exceptions.
The manager verifies that time, units, task progress, and assignment history form a credible submission.
Payroll accepts the verified labor record or opens an attributable correction instead of overwriting the submitted values.
Finance allocates accepted labor and activity quantities to block, task, payroll, and management cost records.

decisions.question: Is the proposed crew qualified and available for this work and hazard profile?
Does a field change require pause, reassignment, or schedule revision?
Do time, units, and task progress form a credible, non-conflicting submission?
Does a disputed labor record require an authorized correction?

outputs: Qualified crew schedule and briefing evidence
Attendance, break, time, and unit record
handoffs: Manager work demand to operations coordinator
Coordinator to foreman and labor contractor
Foreman instructions to crew
Field time and progress to manager
Verified labor facts to payroll
Accepted payroll facts to finance and management reporting
approvals: Supervisor verification of work allocation
Payroll authorization of corrections
Contractor labor acceptance under applicable agreement
exceptions: Missing break, time, unit, or block allocation
workarounds: Paper roster or foreman notebook
Verbal reassignment followed by later re-keying
Manual spreadsheet allocation and payroll correction
risks: Field progress used as a false proxy for time
Labor cost charged to wrong block or task
source_anchors: | 5 | Crew scheduling, attendance, task verification, and payroll allocation |
Operations coordination is often hidden inside other titles, but its work is visible everywhere.
cost studies assume tracking of labor, custom work, materials, and equipment activity
The largest unknowns are behavioral rather than structural.
field_validation_questions: How do crew, employee, contractor, time, piece-rate, and block allocation records relate in each jurisdiction?
Which field progress claims may supervisors verify without changing worker-reported time?
How are heat, rest, meal, language, and training obligations recorded in practice?
How often and why are payroll or costing records corrected after submission?

workflows.name: Materials, purchasing, equipment readiness, and repair
workflows.purpose: Ensure approved field work has compliant materials and serviceable equipment in the required place and window while retaining purchase, use, failure, and repair accountability.
workflows.frequency: Continuous readiness review with per-purchase, staging, failure, and repair events
triggers: Approved work creates material or equipment demand
inputs: Approved work plan and timing window
Material, PPE, equipment, calibration, and container requirements
Inventory, maintenance, and availability status
Approved vendor, product, budget, and certification constraints
Failure report, affected work, and repair evidence
preconditions: Demand is tied to accountable work and timing
Restricted or certification-sensitive purchases are checked before commitment
Equipment identity and service status are known

steps.description: A material, PPE, equipment, calibration, service, or contractor need is recorded against the affected work and timing window.
The coordinator checks inventory, readiness, compliance, vendor, rental, repair, and substitution options.
The manager approves, revises, escalates, or defers the resource commitment within budget and priority limits.
Purchasing places the approved order or service commitment with required product, lot, delivery, and constraint details.
Purchasing records receipt, lot or asset identity, quantity, condition, cost reference, and any discrepancy.
The coordinator stages the received resource for the specific work while preserving custody, calibration, and readiness context.
The foreman verifies that the staged resource is safe, compliant, calibrated where applicable, and ready for release.
Finance reconciles the receipt, parts, service, labor, and resource use with purchasing and work-cost records.

decisions.question: Are required materials, PPE, equipment, and service capacity available in time?
Does the proposed material or vendor satisfy regulatory, buyer, and certification constraints?
Is the resource commitment approved within budget and operational priority?
Can safe partial work remain complete while failed equipment is isolated?

outputs: Approved purchase, rental, or contractor commitment
Received and staged material or equipment
handoffs: Approved work to operations coordinator
Coordinator to purchasing, vendor, contractor, or mechanic
Received resources to foreman and crew
Operator failure report to foreman, manager, and mechanic
Repair evidence back to work release
Purchase and repair actuals to finance
approvals: Budget and priority approval for purchases, rentals, and contractors
Compliance approval for restricted or certification-sensitive products
Qualified return-to-service release
exceptions: Material or PPE out of stock
Equipment failure during a narrow work window
workarounds: Borrow, rent, or book a custom operator
Use an approved alternative
Split or reschedule work
Manual purchase request and emergency phone coordination
risks: Purchase, use, and cost records disconnected
source_anchors: | 7 | Purchasing, materials staging, and equipment readiness |
Someone has to book custom operators, ensure bins are available
manage or request PPE and materials, get equipment repaired
Budget studies and manager postings both imply the need to coordinate tools, fuel, trellis supplies, sprayer materials, and repairs continuously.
field_validation_questions: Which resource checks are actually performed before daily dispatch and seasonal peaks?
Who may authorize emergency purchases, rentals, substitutions, and contractors?
What repair evidence is sufficient to release equipment back to work?
How are inventory lots, field use, invoices, and certification records reconciled?

workflows.name: Grower onboarding, contract-fruit coordination, and relationship history
workflows.purpose: Establish shared identity, terms, quality expectations, reporting, and decision channels so contract fruit can move through the season and harvest without losing cross-organization accountability.
workflows.frequency: Onboarding and annual renewal with recurring visits, reports, decisions, delivery, dispute, and settlement events
triggers: New grower, vineyard, block, or contract
Crop estimate, spray, maturity, or quality update due
inputs: Grower and organization identity
Vineyard, block, row, variety, acreage, and alias history
Contract tonnage, pricing, quality, pick authority, delivery, adjustment, and payment terms
Spray history, certifications, buyer specifications, and reporting cadence
Crop estimates, maturity samples, visits, delivery records, and prior disputes
preconditions: The grower and winery agree which stable block each alias denotes
Required history and compliance evidence is available or explicitly missing
Decision, notice, dispute, and delivery contacts and authority are known

steps.description: Grower, organization, vineyard, block, contract, spray, certification, and quality information is assembled without assuming identifiers already agree.
Grower-relations and compliance verify stable identity mappings, required history, contacts, and onboarding evidence.
The accountable parties record accepted authority, quality, notice, delivery, adjustment, dispute, and payment terms.
Grower and winery participants exchange visits, guidance, crop and maturity reports, restrictions, schedules, and acknowledgements against the active relationship.
The relationship owner confirms that seasonal reporting, delivery, quality, and agreed obligations are fulfilled or explicitly excepted.
Finance and grower-relations reconcile delivery, commercial adjustment, dispute, and settlement history across both organizations.
The completed relationship version becomes historical while renewal, amendment, suspension, or ending creates a separately attributable successor state.

decisions.question: Do grower, winery, compliance, and accounting identifiers resolve to the same operational block?
Is required block, spray, certification, and quality history sufficient for onboarding?
Are authority, quality, notice, delivery, adjustment, dispute, and payment terms mutually understood?
Does an operating issue require guidance, corrective action, commercial adjustment, or dispute escalation?

outputs: Stable grower-vineyard-block relationship and alias history
Active contract and operating responsibility model
handoffs: Grower to grower-relations and compliance
Grower-relations to viticulture, winemaking, intake, transportation, and finance
Winery specifications and timing back to grower
Delivery and quality disposition to contract administration and settlement
approvals: Authorized contract activation or renewal
Shared acknowledgment of pick authority and operating terms
Commercial adjustment or dispute resolution
exceptions: Missing or unacceptable spray or certification history
Notice arrives too late for corrective action
Delivery, downgrade, rejection, or payment dispute
workarounds: Manual map books and alias matching
Phone, email, and vineyard-visit coordination
Conditional onboarding pending evidence
Commercial negotiation or alternative buyer arrangement
risks: Wrong block tied to sample, delivery, or contract
Relationship knowledge held by one person
source_anchors: | 8 | Grower onboarding, contract administration, and relationship history |
A key practical distinction is between estate-grown blocks and contract fruit.
Grower-relations work sits at the boundary between viticulture, procurement, quality assurance, and operations.
quality expectations, spray history, crop estimates, maturity sampling, and harvest schedules must be aligned across two organizations
field_validation_questions: Which onboarding evidence is mandatory before a contract or delivery can proceed?
How are block aliases proposed, disputed, and governed across grower and winery systems?
Which communications require acknowledgment or formal notice?
Where does relationship history live when grower-relations staff change?

workflows.name: Certification, audit preparation, corrective action, and evidence
workflows.purpose: Turn ordinary field and office activity into complete certification evidence, submit it for review, resolve findings, and preserve an auditable annual history.
workflows.frequency: Continuous evidence capture with annual submissions, periodic audits, and finding-driven corrective action
triggers: Questionnaire, plan, diary, or submission becomes due
inputs: Applicable certification standards, regulation, and buyer requirements
Stable site, vineyard, field, block, buffer, and alias identity
Management plans, questionnaires, training, biosecurity, and worker records
Spray, water, nitrogen, material, equipment-cleaning, transport, and buffer-crop records
Prior audit findings and corrective actions
preconditions: Applicable programs, sites, blocks, and responsibility boundaries are known
Operational records retain required detail and attribution
Evidence from contractors and outside growers can be obtained and linked

steps.description: The compliance coordinator maps the active standard and vintage to each site, block, activity, contractor, and buyer relationship.
Evidence owners are assigned to collect ordinary operational records, plans, training, separation, cleaning, transport, input, and annual measurement evidence.
The coordinator checks completeness, attribution, applicability, and internal consistency before preparing the package.
The vineyard manager attests to the prepared package and authorizes submission without replacing independent audit authority.
The auditor reviews submitted and observed evidence against the identified standard and records compliant, evidence-needed, or noncompliant findings.
The auditor records a compliant result when ordinary submitted and observed evidence satisfies the applicable standard.
The certifier issues the certification decision for the compliant review without implying corrective action occurred.
The completed certification cycle remains immutable and is archived when a new standard vintage or annual cycle becomes current.

decisions.question: Which requirements and overlays apply to each site, block, crop, contractor, and buyer relationship?
outcomes: Requirement does not apply with rationale

decisions.question: Is the evidence package complete, attributable, and defensible for submission?
Does submitted and observed evidence satisfy the applicable standard?
Does corrective evidence resolve the finding?

outputs: Annual questionnaire, plan, diary, and audit package
Attributed evidence gap and finding inventory
records: Organic field history and buffer map
handoffs: Certification requirements to manager, field, office, contractor, and grower roles
Operational records to compliance coordinator
Prepared package to manager and certifier
Audit finding to accountable operational owner
Corrective evidence back to certifier
Certification status to winery customers and management
approvals: Manager attestation or submission authorization where required
Independent certifier or auditor decision
Verified closure of corrective actions
exceptions: Missing, late, contradictory, or buyer-specific record
Equipment or transport cleaning evidence missing
workarounds: Late manual assembly from scattered records
Separate buyer or program diaries
Request affidavits or contractor evidence after work
Open corrective action and resubmit evidence
risks: Inability to prove ordinary work met the standard
Operator remains responsible for contractor contamination
Annual package masks unresolved operational gaps
source_anchors: ## Certification overlays, exception inventory, digital opportunity map, field research guide, and sources
Compliance and sustainability personnel are best understood as workflow integrators, not mere record archivists.
SWNZ requires annual questionnaires, site management plans, full spray diaries, a biosecurity register, and periodic audits
where noncompliance appears, certification is withheld until corrective actions are completed.
field_validation_questions: Which programs, buyers, sites, and blocks are in scope and how does that scope change annually?
Which evidence can be generated from operations and which must remain an external authoritative submission?
Who owns each corrective action and who may verify closure?
How are contractor, grower, cleaning, buffer, and preparation records collected and audited?
